sections.title: Types of samples
sections.text: Two types of samples should be included in monitoring programs: (1) Environmental samples collected from non food contact surface areas such as external parts of equipment, floors surrounding the line, pipeline and platforms. In this case, the risk of contamination will depend on the location and design of the processing line and equipment as well as on the levels determined. (2) Samples (line samples) collected from food contact surfaces inside the equipment located after the dryer and prior to packaging and which present a higher risk of directly contaminating the product. Examples of such areas are sifter tailings where product lumps will accumulate and which may be indicative of moisture uptake. The presence of indicator microorganisms, E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) or Salmonella on food contact surfaces represents a very high risk of directly contaminating the product.

sections.title: Target organisms
sections.text: While Salmonella and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species) are the main target organisms, industry has found it advantageous to include EB as indicators of process hygiene. Their levels are good indicators of conditions supporting the potential presence of Salmonella and the potential for growth of Salmonella and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species).

sections.title: Sampling locations and number of samples
path_titles: Sampling locations and number of samples
sections.text: The number of samples will vary with the complexity of the process and processing lines. Preferential locations for sampling should focus on areas where harbourage or entry leading to contamination is likely to occur. Information on appropriate locations can be found in the published literature and can be based on process experience and expertise, or on historical data gathered through plant surveys. Sampling locations should be reviewed on a regular basis and additional ones may need to be included in the program, depending on special situations such as major maintenance or construction activities or where there is any observed indication of poor hygiene. Care should be taken not to introduce a bias in the time samples are taken. This includes ensuring that there is adequate sampling of all manufacturing shifts and production periods within these shifts. Additional samples just prior to start-up are good indices of the effectiveness of cleaning operations.

sections.title: Frequency of sampling
sections.text: The frequency of environmental sampling for the different parameters should be based primarily on factors outlined under (a). It should be defined based on existing data on the presence of relevant microorganisms in the areas submitted to such a monitoring program. In the absence of such information, sufficient suitable data should be generated to correctly define the appropriate frequency. Such data should be collected over sufficiently long periods of time so as to provide representative and reliable information on the prevalence and occurrence of Salmonella over time, and/or E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species), where appropriate. The frequency of the environmental monitoring program needs to be adjusted according to the findings and their significance in terms of risk of contamination. In particular, the detection of pathogens and/or increased levels of indicator organisms in the finished product should lead to increased environmental and investigational sampling to identify the contamination sources. The frequency also needs to be increased in situations where an increased risk of contamination can be expected, e.g., in case of maintenance or construction activities or following wet cleaning activities.

sections.title: Sampling tools and techniques
sections.text: It is important to choose and adapt the type of sampling tools and techniques to the type of surfaces and sampling locations. For example, scrapings of residues or residues from vacuum cleaners provide useful samples, and humidified sponges (or dry swabs) may be more appropriate for larger surfaces.

sections.title: Analytical methods
sections.text: The analytical methods used to analyse environmental samples should be suitable for the detection of the target organisms. Considering the characteristics of environmental samples it is important to demonstrate that the methods are able to detect, with acceptable sensitivity, the target organisms. This should be documented appropriately. Under certain circumstances, it may be possible to composite (pool) certain samples without losing the required sensitivity. However, in the case of positive findings additional testing will be necessary to determine the location of the positive sample. Fingerprinting isolates by one or more of the available genetic techniques (e.g., pulsed-field gel electrophoresis) can potentially provide very useful information about the source(s) of E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species) and pathway(s) that lead to contamination of PF.

sections.title: Data management
sections.text: The monitoring program should include a system to record the data and their evaluation, e.g. performing trend analyses. A continual review of the data is important to revise and adjust monitoring programs. For EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species), it can also reveal low level, intermittent contamination that may otherwise go unnoticed.

sections.title: Actions in case of positive results
path_titles: (i) Actions in case of positive results
sections.text: The purpose of the monitoring program is to find target organisms if present in the environment. Decision criteria and responses based on these monitoring programs should be articulated prior to the establishment of the program. The plan should define the specific action to be taken and the rationale. This could range from no action (no risk of contamination), to intensified cleaning, to source tracing (increased environmental testing), to review of hygienic practices up to holding and testing of product. Generally manufacturers should expect to find EB and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species) in the processing environment. Therefore an appropriate action plan should be designed and established to adequately respond where decision criteria are exceeded. A review of hygiene procedures and controls should be considered. The manufacturer should address each positive result of Salmonella and evaluate changes in the trends of E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species) and EB counts; the type of action will depend upon the likelihood of contaminating the product with Salmonella and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species).

sections.title: MICROBIOLOGICAL MONITORING IN POWDERED FORMULA PREPARATION UNITS
path_titles: MICROBIOLOGICAL MONITORING IN POWDERED FORMULA PREPARATION UNITS
sections.text:
The extrinsic microbiological contamination of powdered formulae during preparation is a factor which needs to be taken into consideration in the design of preventive measures in health care and child care facilities. Such measures are based, as in the case of the manufacture of the powdered formulae, on the application of Good Hygienic Practices as relevant for any establishment handling foods (Recommended International Code of Practice – General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and on the application of HACCP or similar systems to address specific hazards. Such extrinsic microbiological contamination can occur either from the preparation environment, from preparation surfaces, and/or from utensils used during preparation. It is therefore important to assess and verify that the implemented measures are effective. Microbiological monitoring of powdered formula storage areas, preparation areas, and surfaces in direct contact with the product (e.g., utensils) represents an essential element of the quality assurance program. Results from a properly designed monitoring program will assist in identifying potential sources of contamination and in demonstrating the efficacy of cleaning and disinfections procedures. As for section 1 of this annex, a number of factors should be considered when developing the sampling program to ensure its effectiveness, including the target organisms, types of samples, sampling locations, number of samples, frequency of sampling and tools and techniques, analytical methods, data management and actions to take in case of positive results. A monitoring program of PF preparation units is best achieved through sampling and testing of environmental samples for relevant microorganisms such as Salmonella and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species) or hygiene indicators such as EB. It should include swabs from surfaces of preparation areas, sinks, equipment and utensils used as well as residues, for example from vacuum cleaners, collected in the area. It is important that the sampling be done using appropriate sampling tools and techniques, adapted to the type of surfaces and location, and from relevant sites which may, if contaminated, lead to (extrinsic) contamination of PF. The analytical methods used should be suitable for the detection of the target organisms. Considering the characteristics of samples, it is important to demonstrate that the methods are able to detect, with acceptable sensitivity, the target organisms. This should be documented appropriately. Under certain circumstances, it may be possible to composite (pool) certain samples without losing the required sensitivity. However, in the case of positive findings additional testing will be necessary to determine the location of the positive sample. Fingerprinting isolates by one or more of the available genetic techniques (e.g., pulsed -field gel electrophoresis) can potentially provide very useful information about the source(s) of E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) and pathway(s) that lead to contamination of PF. It is important as well to document sampling activities and to include a system to record the data and their evaluation, e.g., performing trend analyses, and to use the data to initiate corrective actions where necessary. For this purpose, it is important to define targets to be achieved, e.g., in terms of acceptable levels of hygiene indicators or absence of pathogens. Such targets should be based on historical data or, if not available, on an initial survey that would permit one to define the normal microbiological status of the different sampling points. For EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species), it can also reveal low level, intermittent contamination that may otherwise go unnoticed. The purpose of the monitoring program is to find target organisms, if they are present. Generally, it is expected that EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species) would be present in the preparation room environment. Decision criteria and responses based on the monitoring program should be articulated prior to the establishment of the program. The plan should define the specific action to be taken where decision criteria are exceeded and the rationale for such action. Each positive result for Salmonella and E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) should be addressed and changes in the trends of EB counts should be evaluated. The

type of action will depend upon the likelihood of contaminating the formulae with Salmonella and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species).  This could range from no action (no  risk of contaminatio n), to intensified cleaning, to source tracing, to the review of hygienic practices.   It is also important to review the monitoring program on a regular basis to take into account changes in the set-up, trends, etc.

It is important that the sampling be done using appropriate sampling tools and techniques, adapted to the type of surfaces and location, and from relevant sites which may, if contaminated, lead to (extrinsic) contamination of PF. The analytical methods used should be suitable for the detection of the target organisms. Considering the characteristics of samples, it is important to demonstrate that the methods are able to detect, with acceptable sensitivity, the target organisms. This should be documented appropriately. Under certain circumstances, it may be possible to composite (pool) certain samples without losing the required sensitivity. However, in the case of positive findings additional testing will be necessary to determine the location of the positive sample. Fingerprinting isolates by one or more of the available genetic techniques (e.g., pulsed -field gel electrophoresis) can potentially provide very useful information about the source(s) of E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) and pathway(s) that lead to contamination of PF. It is important as well to document sampling activities and to include a system to record the data and their evaluation, e.g., performing trend analyses, and to use the data to initiate corrective actions where necessary. For this purpose, it is important to define targets to be achieved, e.g., in terms of acceptable levels of hygiene indicators or absence of pathogens. Such targets should be based on historical data or, if not available, on an initial survey that would permit one to define the normal microbiological status of the different sampling points. For EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species), it can also reveal low level, intermittent contamination that may otherwise go unnoticed. The purpose of the monitoring program is to find target organisms, if they are present. Generally, it is expected that EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species) would be present in the preparation room environment. Decision criteria and responses based on the monitoring program should be articulated prior to the establishment of the program. The plan should define the specific action to be taken where decision criteria are exceeded and the rationale for such action. Each positive result for Salmonella and E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) should be addressed and changes in the trends of EB counts should be evaluated. The

type of action will depend upon the likelihood of contaminating the formulae with Salmonella and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species).  This could range from no action (no  risk of contaminatio n), to intensified cleaning, to source tracing, to the review of hygienic practices.   It is also important to review the monitoring program on a regular basis to take into account changes in the set-up, trends, etc.

page_spans.text: The extrinsic microbiological contamination of powdered formulae during preparation is a factor which needs to be taken into consideration in the design of preventive measures in health care and child care facilities. Such measures are based, as in the case of the manufacture of the powdered formulae, on the application of Good Hygienic Practices as relevant for any establishment handling foods (Recommended International Code of Practice – General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and on the application of HACCP or similar systems to address specific hazards. Such extrinsic microbiological contamination can occur either from the preparation environment, from preparation surfaces, and/or from utensils used during preparation. It is therefore important to assess and verify that the implemented measures are effective. Microbiological monitoring of powdered formula storage areas, preparation areas, and surfaces in direct contact with the product (e.g., utensils) represents an essential element of the quality assurance program. Results from a properly designed monitoring program will assist in identifying potential sources of contamination and in demonstrating the efficacy of cleaning and disinfections procedures. As for section 1 of this annex, a number of factors should be considered when developing the sampling program to ensure its effectiveness, including the target organisms, types of samples, sampling locations, number of samples, frequency of sampling and tools and techniques, analytical methods, data management and actions to take in case of positive results. A monitoring program of PF preparation units is best achieved through sampling and testing of environmental samples for relevant microorganisms such as Salmonella and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species) or hygiene indicators such as EB. It should include swabs from surfaces of preparation areas, sinks, equipment and utensils used as well as residues, for example from vacuum cleaners, collected in the area. It is important that the sampling be done using appropriate sampling tools and techniques, adapted to the type of surfaces and location, and from relevant sites which may, if contaminated, lead to (extrinsic) contamination of PF. The analytical methods used should be suitable for the detection of the target organisms. Considering the characteristics of samples, it is important to demonstrate that the methods are able to detect, with acceptable sensitivity, the target organisms. This should be documented appropriately. Under certain circumstances, it may be possible to composite (pool) certain samples without losing the required sensitivity. However, in the case of positive findings additional testing will be necessary to determine the location of the positive sample. Fingerprinting isolates by one or more of the available genetic techniques (e.g., pulsed -field gel electrophoresis) can potentially provide very useful information about the source(s) of E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) and pathway(s) that lead to contamination of PF. It is important as well to document sampling activities and to include a system to record the data and their evaluation, e.g., performing trend analyses, and to use the data to initiate corrective actions where necessary. For this purpose, it is important to define targets to be achieved, e.g., in terms of acceptable levels of hygiene indicators or absence of pathogens. Such targets should be based on historical data or, if not available, on an initial survey that would permit one to define the normal microbiological status of the different sampling points. For EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species), it can also reveal low level, intermittent contamination that may otherwise go unnoticed. The purpose of the monitoring program is to find target organisms, if they are present. Generally, it is expected that EB and E. sakazakii( Cronobacter species) would be present in the preparation room environment. Decision criteria and responses based on the monitoring program should be articulated prior to the establishment of the program. The plan should define the specific action to be taken where decision criteria are exceeded and the rationale for such action. Each positive result for Salmonella and E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) should be addressed and changes in the trends of EB counts should be evaluated. The

type of action will depend upon the likelihood of contaminating the formulae with Salmonella and E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species).  This could range from no action (no  risk of contaminatio n), to intensified cleaning, to source tracing, to the review of hygienic practices.   It is also important to review the monitoring program on a regular basis to take into account changes in the set-up, trends, etc.

sentences.original: There are four routes by which E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species) and Salmonella can enter PF: 1) through the ingredients added in dry mixing operations during the manufacturing of PF, 2) through contamination of the formula from the processing environment in the steps during or following the drying, 3) through contamination of the PF after the package is opened, and 4) through contamination during or aft er reconstitution by the caregiver prior to feeding.

sentences.original: In addition: Due to the ability of Salmonella and E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) to persist in harbourage sites for prolonged periods of time, processing equipment should be designed, constructed and maintained to avoid, for example, cracks, crevices, rough welds, hollow tubes and structures, close fittings, metal-to-metal or metal-to-plastic surfaces, interfaces between floors and equipment, inadequately installed and maintained insulations, worn seals or other sites that cannot be reached during cleaning.

sentences.original: For wet-mix process: The heat treatment is a key step in ensuring the safety of PF and is therefore considered a CCP.

classification.sentence: These heat-treatments are considered as CCPs and therefore procedures must be in place to detect deviations, such as temperature drops and insufficient treatment times, and to take appropriate corrective measures such as the redirection of the product to waste or reprocessing.
classification.function: Mandating procedures for CCP monitoring and corrective measures
classification.pattern: [Process steps] are CCPs and procedures must be in place to detect deviations and take corrective measures

components.deviations: temperature drops and insufficient treatment times
components.corrective measures: redirection of the product to waste or reprocessing

classification.justification: Typical CCP monitoring and corrective action obligation; partial template match.

sentences.original: Refer to the Recommended International Code of Practice – General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969).

classification.sentence: In addition: As PF is regularly traded internationally, the Principles and Guidelines for the Exchange of Information in Food Safety Emergency Situations (CXG 19-1995), the Principles and Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejection of Imported Food (CXG 25-1997), Principles for Traceability/Product Tracing as a Tool Within a Food Inspection and Certification System (CXG 60-2006) and International Health Regulation (WHA, 2005) should be used in the event of a product recall.
classification.function: Mandate to use specified international principles and guidelines in product recall events
classification.pattern: [Actor] should use [Guidelines] in the event of [Event].

components.Actor: the Principles and Guidelines for the Exchange of Information in Food Safety Emergency Situations (CXG 19-1995), the Principles and Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejection of Imported Food (CXG 25-1997), Principles for Traceability/Product Tracing as a Tool Within a Food Inspection and Certification System (CXG 60-2006) and International Health Regulation (WHA, 2005)
components.Guidelines: the Principles and Guidelines for the Exchange of Information in Food Safety Emergency Situations (CXG 19-1995), the Principles and Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejection of Imported Food (CXG 25-1997), Principles for Traceability/Product Tracing as a Tool Within a Food Inspection and Certification System (CXG 60-2006) and International Health Regulation (WHA, 2005)

classification.justification: Obligation to use multiple named guidelines in recall events; partial match to procedural compliance templates.

sentences.original: Such a monitoring program could be used to assess control of the processing plant environment in the high hygiene areas (dry areas) where contamination might take place, and, thus, would be an essential food safety management tool.

classification.sentence: In order to design an appropriate monitoring program, it is important to understand the ecology of Salmonella and E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) as well as the ecology of EB (used as indicators of process hygiene).
classification.function: Mandate understanding for design
classification.pattern: In order to design [Program], it is important to understand [Subjects]

components.Subjects: the ecology of Salmonella and E. sakazakii (Cronobacter species) as well as the ecology of EB (used as indicators of process hygiene)

classification.justification: The sentence mandates understanding of ecology to design a monitoring program; obligation modality.

pages.title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR POWDERED FORMULAE FOR INFANTS AND YOUNG CHILDREN
pages.summary: Página apresenta o título do documento, cabeçalho recurrente de página e uma seção INTRODUCTION de nível 1 com texto explicativo e listagem. A seção começa na página e continua provavelmente para as seguintes páginas, sem subdivisões adicionais nesta página.

A página contém a continuação da seção INTRODUCTION de nível 1 iniciada na página anterior, com texto explicativo extenso sobre infecções em lactentes relacionadas à fórmula infantil em pó. A seção continua para páginas seguintes, sem subdivisões detectadas nesta página.

A página contém a continuação da seção INTRODUCTION de nível 1 iniciada na página anterior, cujo título visual não está nesta página, mas se percebe continuidade textual. Apresenta também a seção SECTION I. – OBJECTIVES com texto explicativo completo. Inicia a seção SECTION II. – SCOPE, USE AND DEFINITIONS com subtítulo 2.1 SCOPE e seu conteúdo descritivo. A seção II continua para as próximas páginas.
A página apresenta a seção 2.1.2 ROLES OF GOVERNMENTS, INDUSTRY, AND CONSUMERS com seu conteúdo explicativo completo, sem indicações de continuidade para outras páginas.

A página contém dois nós estruturais principais de nível 1: 2.2 USE e 2.3 DEFINITIONS, ambos com seus respectivos textos associados. Não há indicações de continuidade de conteúdo entre páginas e não há título principal do documento visível.

A página apresenta duas seções principais de nível 1: SECTION III – PRIMARY PRODUCTION e SECTION IV – ESTABLISHMENT: DESIGN AND FACILITIES. A seção IV contém um conjunto detalhado de recomendações sobre instalações e equipamentos, mapeado como subtítulo de nível 2, sem título numerado, pois trata-se de conteúdo explicativo. Não há indicação de continuidade entre páginas, e o cabeçalho da página foi identificado como 'CXC  66 - 2008  Page 6 of 29'.
Página contendo as seções 4.1 LOCATION e 4.2 PREMISES AND ROOMS com seus respectivos subtítulos e textos associados. A seção 4.2.1 Design and layout continua na próxima página.

Página 8 do documento contém continuidade da seção 4.2 PREMISES AND ROOMS iniciada na página anterior, com subseção 4.2.3 Temporary/mobile premises and vending machines. Apresenta também a seção 4.3 EQUIPMENT com seus subtítulos 4.3.1, 4.3.2 e 4.3.3. Inicia a seção 4.4 FACILITIES com o subtítulo 4.4.1 Water supply que continua na próxima página. O cabeçalho da página é identificado e o texto está associado corretamente aos respectivos títulos, com marcas de continuidade entre páginas.

A página mapeia subtítulos da seção 4.4 FACILITIES, especificamente itens numerados de 4.4.2 a 4.4.6. Cada subtítulo tem seu texto explicativo associado e os títulos aparecem claramente na página. Não foram detectadas continuações para a próxima página nem conteúdos vindos da anterior. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído do texto. A estrutura está clara para uso em RAG.

A página apresenta seções e subseções organizadas a partir do título em caixa alta SECTION V e subsequentes títulos numerados 5.x e 5.x.x. As seções 4.4.7 e 4.4.8 são concluídas com texto associativo claro. A seção V é estruturada com seus tópicos 5.1, 5.2 e suas subseções até nível 4. A maior parte do texto foi associada corretamente aos seus respectivos nós. Alguns nós indicam continuidade para a próxima página, especialmente 5.2.2 e 5.2.2.1. O cabeçalho da página está identificado e excluído do texto. Não há conteúdos vindos da página anterior nesta página.

A página 11 apresenta uma seção principal numerada 5.2.2 com quatro subseções numeradas 5.2.2.1 a 5.2.2.4. A seção 5.2.2 continua da página anterior (is_continuation_from_previous_page=true) e também continua para a próxima página (continues_on_next_page=true) no principal nó 5.2.2. A subseção 5.2.2.1 está marcada como continuação da página anterior, indicando que seu conteúdo já vinha da página 10. Os textos estão associados aos seus respectivos títulos, respeitando a hierarquia e a ordem natural da página. O cabeçalho repetitivo foi identificado e excluído do texto para a extração.

A página 12 continua a seção 5.2.2 iniciada anteriormente, com subseções 5.2.2.5 a 5.2.2.7 estruturadas como níveis 2. O texto está associado adequadamente às subseções sem incluir cabeçalhos repetitivos. O nó principal 5.2.2 está marcado como continuação da página anterior e continua na próxima, com título não exibido visualmente nesta página.

A página apresenta dois tópicos principais de nível 1: 5.2.3 Microbiological testing and environmental sampling e 5.2.4 Microbiological cross-contamination. O primeiro contém texto explicativo focado em planos de amostragem e monitoramento microbiológico, sem continuação para outras páginas. O segundo tópico começa nesta página e continua para a próxima, incluindo detalhamento dos fatores de contaminação e cuidados ambientais. Não há título da seção 5.2.3 visualmente abreviado nem remanescentes da página anterior. O cabeçalho da página é identificado e excluído do texto dos nós.

A página apresenta seções de nível 1 numeradas de 5.2.5 a 5.7, cada uma com seus respectivos textos, todas sem continuidade entre páginas. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído dos textos dos nós.

A página apresenta uma seção de nível 1 numerada 5.8 com texto explicativo breve e uma nova seção 6 'SECTION VI. – ESTABLISHMENT: MAINTENANCE AND SANITATION' como nó raiz sem texto, contendo subseções numeradas 6.1 a 6.5. A subseção 6.1 contém uma subseção 6.1.2. A maioria dos textos faz referência a um código de prática internacional e detalha pequenos conteúdos adicionais. O nó 6.5 termina com indicação de continuação para a próxima página. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído dos textos dos nós. Não há continuidade da página anterior.

A página apresenta 3 seções de nível 1: SECTION VII, SECTION VIII e SECTION IX, todos com textos que fazem referência a códigos internacionais de higiene, com SECTION IX contendo texto explicativo extenso sobre segurança em fórmulas infantis. SECTION IX tem continuidade da página anterior, indicado pela ausência de novo início e conteúdo denso. O cabeçalho foi identificado e excluído dos textos dos nós. Não há indicações claras de continuação para a próxima página.

A página apresenta a seção principal SECTION IX que continua da página anterior (is_continuation_from_previous_page=true). Dentro dela, há três subseções principais: LOT IDENTIFICATION, e um agrupamento das seções PRODUCT INFORMATION e LABELLING devido à proximidade e continuidade temática no texto. Não há indicações de continuação para página seguinte. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído dos textos dos nós. Texto interno está limpo e adequado para uso em RAG.

A página contém a seção 9.4 EDUCATION, que continua da página anterior e continua para a próxima página, e a seção principal SECTION X – TRAINING como nova seção nesta página. Texto interno está limpo e adequado para RAG. Cabeçalho identificado e excluído do texto.

pages.title: CXC  66 - 2008
pages.summary: A página apresenta a seção principal ANNEX I com um subtítulo Criteria for pathogenic microorganisms. O texto descritivo está associado adequadamente a cada nó. Não há indicação de continuidade entre páginas para estas seções. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído do conteúdo dos nós.

A página contém uma seção principal "Criteria for process hygiene" sem subtítulos claros. O conteúdo detalha critérios microbiológicos para avaliação da higiene do processo. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído dos textos. Não há indicação de continuidade com outras páginas para esta seção.

A página mapeia a seção principal 'CRITERIA FOR PROCESS HYGIENE', que continua da página anterior e segue para a próxima página. Não foram detectados subtítulos nesta página. O texto corresponde ao conteúdo explicativo da seção, excluindo o cabeçalho repetitivo.

pages.title: CXC  66 - 2008
pages.summary: A página apresenta hierarquia principal do ANNEX II sem numeração, nível 1, com um subtítulo identificado como 'Criteria for pathogenic microorganisms' também nível 1 por caixa alta/estrutura. O conteúdo textual é associado apropriadamente a cada nó, respeitando a continuidade para a próxima página no ANNEX II.

Página 23 continua a seção principal 'MICROBIOLOGICAL CRITERIA FOR HYGIENE CONTROL' sem subdivisões adicionais. O conteúdo é extenso e explicativo, focado em critérios microbiológicos para controle higiênico. A seção já vinha da página anterior e não se estende para a próxima página.

A página continua a seção principal já iniciada na página anterior, representada pelo primeiro nó de nível 1 que envolve métodos microbiológicos. Aparece um novo título nível 1, "Labelling and Education", com seu respectivo texto. Há também uma referência bibliográfica no rodapé, identificada aqui como um nó auxiliar sem título visível na página. O conteúdo do primeiro nó é continuidade da página anterior e não se estende para a próxima página.

A página 25 do documento apresenta o Anexo III com o título principal em caixa alta e sem numeração. Sob este, está a seção de nível 1 numerada '1. GUIDANCE FOR THE ESTABLISHMENT OF AN ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING AND PROCESS CONTROL PROGRAM IN HIGH HYGIENE PROCESSING AREAS', que continua da página anterior. Esta seção inclui subseções sem numeração específicas para 'Salmonella', 'E. sakazakii(Cronobacter species)' e 'Enterobacteriaceae', cada uma com seu conteúdo textual. O texto está limpo de cabeçalhos repetitivos e a hierarquia foi estruturada para uso em RAG.

A página 26 contém quatro seções estruturadas de nível 1 identificadas pela numeração (a), (b), (c) e (d). A seção (a) continua da página anterior com texto detalhado e as demais são seções independentes na página atual. Não há subtítulos ou hierarquia adicional nesta página. Texto interno foi associado corretamente a cada seção e o cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído do texto das seções para uso em RAG.

A página 27 contém cinco seções estruturadas de nível 1 identificadas pela numeração entre parênteses (e), (f), (g), (h) e (i). Cada seção possui texto interno explicativo associado e não há subtítulos. O cabeçalho da página foi identificado e excluído do texto das seções para uso em RAG. Não há continuidade de conteúdo com a página anterior nem continuação para a próxima página.

A página contém uma seção principal numerada como 2 em nível 1, com texto explicativo detalhado sobre monitoramento microbiológico em unidades de preparo de fórmula em pó. Não há subtítulos. A seção não continua da página anterior, mas o conteúdo continua para a próxima página. O cabeçalho foi identificado e excluído do texto da seção para RAG.

Contém uma única seção nível 1 numerada como 2, que continua da página anterior. O texto registra instruções sobre ações em monitoramento microbiológico. O título não aparece explicitamente nesta página, mas o conteúdo está associado à seção existente. Não há subtítulos.
